sections.text: Welcome to English One, Module Six — Descriptive Writing. A great description does not just name a thing. It opens your senses and plants you in the room. Today you learn three figurative moves and the five senses that bring a paragraph to life.

Here is the trap. A student writes — The garden was nice. The flowers were pretty. The air smelled good. Three flat lines, no scene. Now read this — Rose petals burned like small lanterns, and the warm air carried jasmine. Same garden. Different camera. The first one names things. The second one lets you stand inside it.

Game plan. First, the five senses — sight, sound, smell, taste, touch — and how to pick details that earn their place. Second, three figurative moves — simile, metaphor, and personification — what each is and how it sounds. Third, two pauses — rewrite a flat sentence, then spot the devices in a real paragraph.

The five senses. Sight is the obvious one — but go past color. Sound — the small ones — a faucet drip, a chair scrape. Smell is the most memory-laden — pencil shavings, wet pavement. Taste lives in food and weather — salt, smoke. Touch is texture and temperature — wool, sand, cold glass. Pick three or more per paragraph.

Three figurative moves. Simile compares with the word like or as — her laugh was like wind chimes. Metaphor states the comparison directly with no like or as — her laugh was wind chimes. Personification gives a human action to a non-human thing — the wind whispered through the trees. Different shapes. Same goal — make the reader feel it.

The most common mistake — clichés instead of fresh images. Cliché — her eyes were as blue as the ocean. The reader has seen it a thousand times. Fresh — her eyes were the blue you only see right after a hard rain. Same color. Different image. Clichés tell the reader you copied. Fresh images tell the reader you actually saw the thing.

Worked example — describe my grandfather's kitchen. Sight — yellow linoleum cracked at the seams. Sound — the refrigerator hummed like a tired old engine. Smell — coffee that had been sitting since dawn. Touch — the chair vinyl stuck to my legs. Personification — the kettle complained on the back burner. Five short pieces. Three senses, one simile, one personification — already a vivid room.

Your turn. On screen — a flat sentence — The classroom was loud. Rewrite it as one sensory image. Use at least two senses and one figurative device — simile, metaphor, or personification. Be specific — name a real sound, a real object. Pause now. Rewrite it. Press play when you are ready.

Time. One possible rewrite — Chairs scraped like the start of an old song, and the air smelled of fresh paper and somebody's tangerine. Sight — chairs. Sound — scrape. Smell — paper and tangerine. Simile — like the start of an old song. The flat version named loud. The sensory version puts you in the third row. Yours will differ — what matters is real detail, more than one sense, and one figurative device.

One more. On screen — a short paragraph about a winter morning. Find and label one simile, one metaphor, and one personification. Use the exact wording from the paragraph. The labels matter — your assignment requires you to underline and label each device. Pause now. Mark the three devices. Press play when ready.

Time. The paragraph — Snow lay like a thick white quilt over the field. The wind argued with the bare branches. Each step was a small bowl of sugar under my boot. Simile — like a thick white quilt. Personification — the wind argued — human action for a non-human force. Metaphor — each step was a small bowl of sugar — direct equation, no like or as. Three devices, one paragraph.

The assignment. Write two descriptive paragraphs — at least one hundred fifty words each. Paragraph one — a place. Paragraph two — a person. Each paragraph needs at least two similes, one metaphor, one personification, and details from at least three senses. Underline every device. Label simile, metaphor, or personification in the margin. Submit clean copy and your marked-up draft. Graded on freshness, not cliché.

Recap. Five senses — pick three or more, and go past sight. Three figurative moves — simile uses like or as, metaphor states it directly, personification gives a human action to a non-human thing. Freshness beats cliché. Specific beats general. Show, do not name.

How to master this. Read the Purdue OWL pages on descriptive writing strategies. Watch Khan Academy on similes, metaphors, and analogies. In your Learn Portal dashboard, draft the two descriptive paragraphs — one place, one person — with every device underlined and labeled. Book an advisor check-in if either pause felt shaky. Next up — Module Seven, Short Story Analysis.
